variables.content: Let $m^{*}$ denote the quasiparticle effective mass at the Fermi level of a Fermi-liquid state, defined operationally via the low-temperature specific heat coefficient $\gamma$ as $\gamma\propto m^{*}$ (so $\gamma=C/T$ as $T\to0$), and let $T_{\rm coh}$ denote the temperature below which Fermi-liquid thermodynamics (linear specific heat and temperature-independent susceptibilities) are observed. The proposition is that, in a conventional Fermi liquid of itinerant quasiparticles, $T_{\rm coh}$ is proportional to an inverse effective-mass scale, i.e., $T_{\rm coh}\propto 1/m^{*}$ up to factors set by band parameters and quasiparticle residue, which is the standard phenomenological relation between effective mass and the energy window of coherent quasiparticle behavior.

Assuming the standard Landau Fermi-liquid relation that the low-temperature linear specific-heat coefficient \gamma is proportional to the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi surface (hence proportional to the quasiparticle effective mass m^{*}), the measured value \gamma=2.11\ \mathrm{K}^{-1} implies an effective-mass ratio m^{*}/m\approx 2.12 (where m is the bare fermion mass) and, under the usual formulas that relate the effective mass renormalization to the first Landau Fermi-liquid parameter, yields F_{1}\approx 3.36.

For a homogeneous three-dimensional electron Fermi liquid with bare electron mass $M$, Landau-quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}$, Fermi momentum $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, and noninteracting density of states at the Fermi level $N_{0}$, and with $F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})$ denoting the $p$-wave ($\ell=1$) component of the Landau interaction amplitude evaluated at equal incoming and outgoing momenta $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, the quasiparticle effective mass satisfies the Landau–Fermi–liquid relation
      $$
      \frac{M^{*}}{M}=\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{N_{0}F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})}{3}}.
      $$
      [8]

Let \gamma denote the Sommerfeld coefficient defined by C_{\mathrm{el}}(T)=\gamma T (units J mol^{-1} K^{-2}). Let N(0) denote the quasiparticle density of states per mole at the Fermi level and let m^{*} denote the quasiparticle effective mass entering the low-energy Fermi-liquid description. In a conventional single-component Fermi liquid the relation \gamma=\tfrac{\pi^{2}}{3}k_{B}^{2}N(0) holds, and for bands whose density of states scales with effective mass one has N(0)\propto m^{*}, so that \gamma\propto m^{*}; therefore, to the extent that a Fermi-liquid description applies to the low-temperature electronic states under consideration, a measured change in \gamma(T) by a factor \sim 5 corresponds approximately to a fivefold change in the quasiparticle effective mass m^{*}.

Let $m>0$ denote the bare particle mass and $m^{*}>0$ denote the Landau effective mass parameter appearing in Fermi-liquid theory, and let $N_{0}=mk_{F}/\pi^{2}$ be the bare normal-state density of states at the Fermi level. The proposition is that the dominant Fermi-liquid effect on the computed superfluid-density correction may be captured, to leading order, by replacing $N_{0}$ with $N_{0}^{*}=m^{*}k_{F}/\pi^{2}$ (equivalently, replacing $m$ by $m^{*}$ in $N_{0}$) in the expressions for $N(0)$ and thus in the coefficient that multiplies $\rho$, with residual Fermi-liquid self-consistency corrections being negligible in the stated regime.
      R. Combescot, J. Phys. C 14, 4765 (1981).

The empirical dimensionless prefactor obtained from the fits, p ≈ 13.6, is substantially larger than the value p = 2π ≈ 6.28 predicted by a simple isotropic three-dimensional effective-mass Fermi-liquid model, and this discrepancy indicates that one or more of the following modify the numerical prefactor entering the Fermi-liquid damping in layered, quasi-two-dimensional TiS₂: anisotropy of the Fermi surface, limited momentum-relaxing umklapp scattering in small Fermi pockets, uncertainties in the effective mass, or disorder/stacking-fault scattering.

For a Fermi liquid with Fermi wave vector k_F (Å^{-1}), define a dimensionless wave-vector-dependent effective mass m^*(k) (measured in units of the bare 3He mass m_3) by choosing a background mass parameter m_0 ≥ 1 and a half-width fraction f > 0, defining the symmetric interval {k: |k−k_F| < f k_F}, setting m^*(k)=m_0 for |k−k_F| ≥ f k_F, and for |k−k_F| < f k_F setting
      $$
      m^{*}(k)=m_{0}+(m^{*}(k_{F})-m_{0})\frac{1+\cos\big(\frac{(k-k_{F})\pi}{f k_{F}}\big)}{2},
      $$
      with the Fermi-surface value specified as m^*(k_F)=2.8. This prescription provides a single smooth cosine interpolation between m_0 and m^*(k_F) over the interval of half-width f k_F and is used as the representation of the effective-mass enhancement in subsequent numerical integrations.

Within Landau Fermi-liquid theory for electronic quasi-particles, let $Z$ denote the quasi-particle spectral weight (the magnitude of the discontinuity in the momentum occupation $n(\mathbf{k})$ at the Fermi vector $k_{\mathrm{F}}$) and let $m^{*}$ and $m$ denote the renormalized and the non-interacting effective masses respectively; then, for a band on which the quasi-particle energy is uniformly scaled relative to a non-interacting reference, the observed relation $E_{\mathrm{exp}}(\mathbf{k})\approx Z\,E_{\mathrm{DFT}}(\mathbf{k})$ implies an effective mass enhancement $m^{*}/m\approx 1/Z$, so that an observed energy renormalization factor $Z\approx 0.5$ corresponds to $m^{*}/m\approx 2$ within the Fermi-liquid framework.

Under the simplifying assumption that the Landau Fermi-liquid parameter F_{2}=0, the shear-viscosity-to-relaxation-time ratio in normal liquid 3He satisfies the Fermi-liquid relation
      $$
      \frac{\eta}{\tau_{\eta}}=\frac{1}{5}\rho p_{F}^{2}\frac{1}{(m^{*}/m)m^{2}},
      $$
      where η is the shear viscosity, τ_{\eta} the shear relaxation time, ρ the mass density, p_{F}=\hbar(3\pi^{2}n)^{1/3} the Fermi momentum with number density n=\rho/m, m the bare particle mass, and m^{*} the quasiparticle effective mass; this relation is invoked to convert measured viscoelastic quantities into an estimate of the effective-mass ratio m^{*}/m.
      [6]

For a homogeneous, isotropic, three-dimensional Fermi liquid with a single-band dispersion near the Fermi surface, the quasiparticle density of states per spin at the Fermi level is given by the standard Landau-Fermi-liquid expression
      $$
      N(E_{F})=\frac{M^{*}p_{F}}{\pi^{2}},
      $$
      where M^{*} is the Landau quasiparticle effective mass (the mass parameter entering the low-energy dispersion near the Fermi momentum), p_{F} is the magnitude of the Fermi momentum, and N(E_{F}) denotes the single-particle density of states at the chemical potential; this expression neglects band-structure anisotropies and multi-band contributions.
